FERNAN-CABALLERO, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 15 m. NNW of Ciudad- Real, partido of Piedrabuena, 3 m. from the r. bank of the Guadiana.
FERNAN-NUNEZ, a small town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 12 m. SSE of Cordova, partido of Rambla. Pop. 3,808. It has considerable woollen and linen manufactures.
FERNAN - VELOSO, a river of Mozambique, which throws itself into the channel of that name 30 m. N of Mozambique.

FERNANDEZ, or Juan Fernandez, two islands in the Pacific ocean, opposite to the coast of Chili, and about 32 leagues distant from each other. One of them, as lying farther off towards the W, is distinguished by the epithet DE AFUERA [Ulloa]; the other, as being nearer the land, is called DE-TIERRA. It is to the latter that the name JUAN FERNANDEZ properly applies,—an appellation which it is supposed to have received from a Spaniard of that name who resided upon it for some time. It is situated in 33° 40' S lat., and 79° W long.; 110 leagues W from the coast of Chili; and about 440 N of Cape-Horn. It is of irregular form, surrounded by a steep shore, about 5 leagues in length from NW to SE, and 2 leagues in breadth. There are three harbours and bays in the island; but two of these, one on the W and another on the E side, are much exposed, and have only about 14 fath. water. The third, which is the largest, and is called Cumberland-bay, lies on the NE coast, and has anchorage in 10 fath. within 2 cables' length of the beach. At a distance the whole island appears like one entire rock, and is for the most part very high land. In the N quarter the mountains are lofty, steep, rugged, and almost inaccessible; but the surface slopes away towards the S point, where a remarkable islet, or large detached rock, appears about half-a-mile from the main land. The loftiest mountain is called Yungue, i. e. 'the Anvil,' from its resemblance to a blacksmith's anvil. It rises 3,000 ft. above a shore formed by an abrupt wall of dark-coloured bare rock 800 or 900 ft. in height. The island is composed of basaltic greenstone and trap.—Upon approaching the coast, deep and romantic valleys are perceived, intersecting the mountainous districts, shaded with different kinds of trees, and covered with rich verdure. The heat of summer is moderate; the winter, which is never severe, lasts only during the months of June and July; and there is little thunder or tempestuous weather at any season. Copious dews at night supply the want of rain, and preserve the trees and herbage in continual verdure. The land in the valleys and on the acclivities of the lower hills is a black fertile mould, capable of producing grain and roots in abundance; but towards the SW, where the surface is low and flat, the soil is loose, dry, and stony. The valleys and N sides of the mountains are covered with trees; but the piercing violence of the S winds prevents their growth on the declivities in that direction. The vegetation is rather Californian than Chilian in its general character. The forest-trees are of various sorts, but many of them afford excellent timber. The myrtles are said to be the largest, and to be capable of yielding planks 40 ft, in length. The pimento and the cabbage-tree are found in some places, but none of the American fruit-trees which grow naturally in the forests. Every part of the island is covered with a sort of oat, which grows to the height of a man; there are vast quantities of rose-bushes; and a great variety of esculent vegetables, especially antiscorbutics, such as purslane, mint, water-cresses, wild sorrel, turnips, and Swedish radishes, are indigenous. Ground-apples, melons, peaches, figs, grapes, strawberries of an enormous size, and cherries, abound. The coasts swarm with sea-wolves or sea-lions, by the Spaniards commonly named Lobos de Aceyte, or 'oilwolves,' because of the vast quantity of fat or blubber of which their body consists, making them appear when they move like a skin full of oil. Vast shoals of fish frequent the shores, particularly cod of a large size and very similar to that of Newfoundland; bream, silver-fish, and lobsters often half-a-yard in length, may be taken in great abundance. The only quadrupeds found on the island are bullocks, dogs, goats, and rats. The original breed of goats, which had been set on shore by Juan-Fernandez, soon became so numerous as to furnish an excellent supply of provisions to the navigators of these seas; the bullocks, dogs, and rats, are additions since his time. The land-birds are not numerous. The advantages which this island afforded for refitting, victualling, wooding, and watering, long rendered it the principal resort of the Buccaneers and other cruizers in those quarters; and in order to deprive them of the supplies which it afforded, the Spanish government sent out a number of dogs, particularly greyhounds, for the purpose of exterminating the goats. Numerous flocks, however, of goats still remain in the steep places of the mountains, where the dogs are unable to pursue them, but where they are equally inaccessible to man. It has been observed of these dogs, that they never bark till they are brought together with others of their species, when they begin to imitate them in a strange manner as if learning a new acquirement. No venomous creatures or beasts of prey have been observed on the island.—On this island, Alexander Selkirk, Dampier's coxswain, resided from the year 1705 to 1709; and from his history Daniel Defoe is understood to have composed his inimitable 'Adventures of Robinson Crusoe.' In this place also, in 1741, Lord Anson recruited the health of his crew, when so debilitated by the scurvy, and exhausted by the storms which they had encountered, as to be scarcely able to muster strength sufficient to heave the anchor. In 1766 the Spaniards formed a settlement and established a garrison on the island. In the year following, Captain Carteret, in the course of his voyage round the world, attempting to enter Cumberland-bay, was surprised to find it in the possession of the Spaniards. In 1792, Lieutenant John Moss of the royal navy, then commanding the ship William employed in the whale and seal fishery, visited both the islands of Fernandez; and some notices of them were published by him in the 'Athenæum' for 1807. He obtained permission to land and supply the wants of his crew; and found about 40 settlers, and a garrison of 6 soldiers, occupying a village pleasantly situated in a fine valley between two high hills. Every house had a garden with arbours of grape-vines; and figs, cherries, plums, and almonds, appeared in a green state. There was abundance also of potatoes, cabbages, onions, thyme, and other vegetables; but none of these vegetables came to perfection, as a kind of grub had been found to destroy the kitchen gardens. About 1814 the Spaniards withdrew the whole of the garrison and settlers; and it seems to have been deserted a long time, until, during the late revolutions, the victorious royalist Osorio, unwilling to shed blood, sent off to the island a cargo of captive patriots collected from all parts of the S. American continent. Afterwards, when the republicans got the upper hand, those exiles were brought back with much exultation and show of triumph, and, in their room, were shipped-off all those who were accused of too strong a leaning to the Spanish rule. A mutiny soon after took place on the island, and the Chilian government deemed it expedient to give over all further attempts to colonize it, fearing that it might プラグラグラグラグラグラグラグラグラグラグラグラグラグ アン・ファン・ファン・ファン・カルファ カルファ アン・ファン・ファン・ファン・ファン・ファン・ファン・ファン・ファン・ファン・ファ FER become at once independent and piratical. The settlement was accordingly broken up, and no more was thought of Juan-F., until an enterprising North American, the captain of a trader, made an agreement with the government whereby he obtained the exclusive right of hunting the wild cattle on the island, and preparing the dried meat called by the Chilians charqui. He kept in employment a number of foreign sailors and Chilians, the former chiefly engaged in killing seals on the adjoining island Mas-àfuera; appeared to make a good business of it; and was in Valparaiso jocosely called 'the King of the Isles.' The constitution promulgated by the President Pinto recognised Juan-F. as an integral part of the republic, but it still remained uninhabited until Don Joaquin Larrain, a man of great wealth, and well-known in Chili, obtained permission to colonize it. At his death the present government took possession of the island, and sent thither a large body of convicts, without the necessary accompaniment of a garrison in which confidence might be placed. The consequence was, that the guards and prisoners joined in revolt, seized a ship, and compelled the captain to carry them to N. Chili; thence they went over the Andes, but were at length seized by the government of San-Juan, sent to Santiago-where their ringleader was shot-and then shipped-off again to the island under a better guard. Thus the government at present derives no further advantage from Juan-F., than that of a comparatively cheap and secure penal establishment; but the maritime importance of the place will probably be recognised ere long. The present settlement upon this island consists of about 100 huts or cottages, "the best of them built of mud and whitewashed, but the greater part only Robinson Crusoe like, -of posts and branches of trees." The governor's house has grated windows, plastered walls, and a roof of red tiles. The worst part of the convicts are locked-up, under sentries, in caves dug into the side of the mountain, nearly half-way up above the village whither they are taken by day, and engaged in public works.—Ships from Europe bound to Peru, and not touching at Chili, usually touch at this island to test their chronometers. FERNANDEZ DE AFUERA lies in 33° 41' S lat., and 81° 40' W long.; both islands are situated nearly in the same lat.; but may be easily distinguished by the circumstance of the land in De Afuera being highest towards the S, while the more elevated part of De Tierra is towards the N; at the S point also of the latter is a large rock or islet about half-a-mile from the island. Afuéra is very high, appearing at a distance like one hill or rock, and in clear weather may be seen from Fernandez-de-Tierra. It is of a triangular form, and about 8 leagues in circumf. All navigators who have visited it concur in their descriptions of the extreme difficulty of landing, on account of the high surf which breaks upon large fragments of rocks all round the island, so that a boat cannot safely come within a cable's length of the shore. The only mode of landing is by swimming from the boat, and then mooring her without the rocks. Neither is there any other way of getting off the wood and water casks, but by hauling them to the boat with ropes; and even this is sometimes found to be impracticable. There is plenty of wood and water all round the island; but the E side has the most pleasant aspect, showing numerous valleys covered with trees, rich in verdure, and abounding in flowers of the lily and violet kinds. Down every valley runs a copious stream of water, which expands in its descent among the rocks into several successive reservoirs. At the period of Captain Moss's visit seals were so numerous on this island, that they literally covered the shores; and his crew took 2,100 of them in a few days. Various kinds of fish also, particularly cod, halibut, coal-fish, and cray-fish, may be taken here. Among the birds were observed various kinds of hawks, some very large, and others as small as a goldfinch; and the pintado-birds were so numerous, that in one night, during a gale, Carteret's people caught not less than 700, which flew straight into a fire kindled on shore. Among the vegetables, the mountain - cabbage was particularly noticed; the trees consisted principally of red cedar, and a hard yellow wood like box. - Byron's Voyage round the World.—Carteret's Voyage round the World.—Ulloa's Voyage to South America, vol. ii.—Athenœum for 1807, vol. i.—Dana.—Voyages of the Adventurer and Beagle.
FERNANDINA, a village of Nassau co., in the state of Florida, U. S., 313 m. E of Tallahassee, in the N part of Amelia island. It has the best harbour S of Chesapeake bay.
FERNANDO (SAN), a headland of the island of Luzon, on the N side of Lingayen gulf, on the W side of the island, in N lat. 16° 31'.

FERNANDO (SAN), a village of Upper California, 129 m. NW of San Diego, on the Pacific.—Also a small town of Mexico, in the prov. and 110 m. NNE of Cohahuila, about 20 m. W of the Rio Bravo-del- Norte.—Also a fort of Costa-Rica, 78 m. ENE of Cartago, on a height near the embouchure of the Matina.-Also a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Tamaulipas, on the l. bank of the Tigre or San Fernando, 35 m. WNW of the entrance of that river into the gulf of Mexico.—Also a town of Venezuela, in the prov. and 189 m. SE of Caracas, on the r. bank of the Chivata, an affluent of the Orinoco, and 195 m. SW of Cumana.—Also a town of Chili, capital of the prov. of Colchagua, on the r. bank of the Tinguaririca, 84 m. S of Santiago, and 120 m. SW of Valparaiso. Pop. 7,500. It has a parish church, a convent, and a college, with a handsome church built by the Jesuits. This town was founded in 1741 by Count Superunda.
FERNANDO (SAN). See TIGRE.
FERNANDO (SAO), a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Goyaz, on the Tocantins, 261 m. S of São-Joãodas-Duas-Barras.

FERNANDO (SAO), or Dolrados, a chain of mountains which runs SSE along the confines of Bolivia and Brazil, between the territory of the Chiquitos and Matto-Grosso, extending from the Campos-Parecis on the N to the Rio Latirequiqui on the S, a distance of 165 m.

FERNANDO-DE-APURE (SAN), a town of Venezuela, in the dep. of Apure, prov. and 165 m. ENE of Varinas, on the r. bank of the Apure, at the confluence of the Portuguesa. Pop. 6,000. It is advantageously situated for trade, but is exposed to excessive heat during the greater part of the year.
FERNANDO-DE-ATABAPO, a town of Spanish Guayana, on the l. bank of the Orinoco, at the confluence of the Atabapo and Guaviari, 155 m. WNW of Esmeralda. FERNANDO-DE-CATAMARCA (San). CATAMARCA.

FERNANDO-DE-NORONHA, an island in the Atlantic, about 70 leagues NE from Cape San Roque, on the coast of Brazil. Many little islets are divided from the principal island, and from each other by narrow channels. These are all rocky and barren, but frequented by vast numbers of sea-fowl. Rat-island is about 1 m. sq. Booby and Egg islands are very small; and Mount St. Michael is a mere rocky islet rising vertically from the sea. The geological structure of F., the main island, consists of basalt, hornblende, and greenstone; but there is no vesicular lava nor scoriæ. Rat and Booby islands FER are composed of limestone. F. is about 7 m. long and 24 m. broad; and about 20 m. in circumf. Its surface is unequal and mountainous. Its centre is formed by a mountain about 1,000 ft. high, called Campanario, or 'the Belfry,' from the resemblance of its summit to a church-tower. The upper part of this mountain is steep, and on one side overhangs its base. The island is in general fertile, producing Indian corn and cotton, and every species of grain and fruit common in hot climates; but, from the want of moisture, the crops are often destroyed. Two or three years often pass without rain; there are no permanent streams, and, excepting in some brooks, not a drop of water is to be found in the island. In the wet season, however, the island is "one continued swamp and bog." [Foster]. The soil is a fine rich loam of considerable depth. The wet season is from January to June. When Ulloa visited this island, no rain had fallen for two years, but violent showers came on soon after, in May. The whole surface is covered with wood; but from the dryness of the climate, there is little appearance of luxuriance [Darwin.]; but when Captain Foster visited it in the month of June, it exhibited "great richness and variety of vegetation." Few of the trees, however, exceed 6 or 7 inches in diam. When the Portuguese had compelled the French East India company to evacuate this island, they erected seven forts to defend it. Three of these defend the N harbour, two the NW, and two the E part of the island, where there is a small bay fit only for barks. The forts are all built of stone, are spacious, and are well provided with large artillery. Fort Remedios alone can contain 1,000 men. The island has long been employed as a place of exile and imprisonment; and is occupied by a garrison of 200 troops, and 800 civil and criminal inhabitants. There are two harbours capable of receiving ships of burden, one on the N, and another on the NW side of the island. The first is the best, for shelter, capacity, and goodness of bottom; but both are exposed to the N and W, and when these winds prevail they are both impracticable; even in E winds it is dangerous to attempt a landing. The animal kingdom is chiefly confined to rats and lizards. The harbours or roads abound in fish of five or six different species, among which are lampreys and morenas of an enormous size. Between the months of December and April, when the turtles lay their eggs, the shores of the whole island are covered with them; they afterwards retire into the sea, and disappear. Flour and provisions are imported; but the inhabitants subsist chiefly upon farina di pari, or 'wood meal,' obtained from roots called moniato, nane, and yuca. It is little more than saw dust, both with regard to taste and smell. The cattle are good and abundant; and excellent poultry are reared. The position of the island, according to solar observations, is N lat. 3° 56′ 20″, and W long. 32° 37′ 45".—Ulloa's Voyage to South America, Book ix.—Cook's Second Voyage,—Foster's Narrative.

FERNANDO PO, or FERNAO-DO-Po, an island situated in the bight of Benin, 10 leagues from the See Calabar river, and 20 from the Bonny, 1,400 m. from Sierra Leone. It received its name from a gentleman in the service of Alphonzo V. of Portugal, who discovered it in 1741. It lies between 3° 6' and 3° 47' N lat.; and between 8° 25' and 8° 57' E long.; and is about 35 m. in length, and from 12 to 22 m. in breadth. It is high, and in many parts precipitous. The channel between it and the mainland is about 19 m. in width, and strikingly picturesque. Its depth varies from 12 to 40 fath. Two high and peaked mountains,—the black sand on the beach,— and the scoriæ and other substances found here which have evidently undergone the action of fire, -denote this island to be of volcanic origin. From the N extremity the land ascends, in a NE direction and gradual slope, to a ridge, rising to the height of 8,000 ft., which connects two peaked mountains, one of which, Clarence peak (d), attains an alt. of 10,700 ft.; the whole surface of this slope is covered with a forest of shrubs and lofty trees of luxuriant growth, even to within 1,000 ft. of the summit of the loftiest peak. A less elevated range at the S end separates Melville-bay and Cape Badgely, and is likewise clothed with verdure to the summit. Beyond the region of wood, and the crest of the hills, the villages of the natives are situated on the side of the mountains as far up as about one-third of their height. Numberless little streams trickle down the sides of the hills into George's bay; and, besides these, three very considerable rivers, at all of which ships may water with the utmost facility, discharge themselves into the bay, one at each extremity, and one about the middle of the bay.—The rainy season lasts from May to December, when it is followed by a season of peculiar dense vapour called 'the smokes.' Although the therm, has been observed to rise to 80° in the afternoon, yet the land and sea breezes give to the atmosphere a freshness quite unknown on the adjacent coast. The range of the therm. in October is from 74° to 78°. The soil is rich, on a substratum of sandstone. The productions are rice, bananas, sugar-cane, cotton, tobacco, ginger, manioc, cocoas, and yams of a remarkably large size and fine flavour. Pease, carrots, and grapes, have been cultivated with success. Besides these vegetable productions, domestic animals, sheep, goats, fowls, and turtle, are numerous, so that the means of subsistence are abundant. There are about 15 native towns and villages on the island, and a pop. of probably 15,000. The Spaniards affected to consider the natives of F. as a ferocious people; a private informant, who visited the island in 1848, says he always found them good-humoured and inoffensive, and that during his stay on the island in his voyage he had not the least occasion to conclude them either treacherous, vindictive, or unchaste. There appear to be two or three races on the island; at least, there are two or more dialects, if not distinct languages, spoken upon it. The Ediyahs, or Būbis, are a fine race of people, with well-formed limbs, and a muscular and active frame. Their average height is 5 ft. 6 in. Their countenances are somewhat peculiar, the general contour of their face being that of a square, with the angle rounded-off. The nose, the lips, and the quick and piercing eye, approach nearer to the features of the European than to those of the African; their cheek-bones are not so high, and their nose is less expanded; and hence some have concluded them to be of Portuguese origin. They have woolly hair, softer and longer than in any of the W. African Negro tribes, and which, being twisted, and thickly daubed with red clay, appears like so many ropes dangling behind them. The hue of their skin is a dark olive, in some nearly jet black, in others inclining to copper colour; but their limbs are always completely besmeared with a mixture of red clay and palm oil, and their faces with a fine pulverized yellow ochre, which gives them the appearance of Mulattoes. The women have generally a soft and rather pleasing expression of countenance, which even the horrid practice of cutting deep scars across the face does not remove. The men wear a flat conical straw-hat; which, in the chief, is oddly adorned with a pair of ram's or goat's horns in front. Married people of both sexes wear a fringe about 9 in. broad, made of a species of rush or of leaves, round their loins; the unmarried neglect all clothing, except perhaps a monkey-skin, or piece of sea- | in 1801, 576; in 1841, 620. FER weed. The women go about in a state of perfect nudity, unconscious of indecency, and free from insult. Indeed no tribe of Africans have such antipathy to European clothing as this singular people. Their language is not less different from those of the continental Negroes than their manner and appearance. They have no communication, of a pacific kind at least, with the opposite coast; neither does it appear that the superstitious veneration of the Fetish, so universal along the W coast of Africa, is at all known in this island. They are Pagans; but their religious rites are untainted by human blood. Each town and v. has its king or chief, who with the head-men settle all disputes. Their weapons are spears made entirely of wood. Their houses are of wicker-work, and all nearly of the same size and plan. They are built round an open area, and each is surrounded with a railed fence or enclosure, within which the cattle of the owner are shut up at night.—The British flag is now hoisted on Clarencemount (a) on the N side of this island: our government, considering the station eligible for the ships employed in putting down the slave-trade, seem disposed to encourage the formation of a settlement upon this island, which has been long abandoned by both Portuguese and Spaniards. Maidstone-bay, on the N coast (c), has a fine shore, and clear of wood. The Barracouta river empties itself into it. Captain Kelly, of the Pheasant sloop of war, who entered a noble bay on its NW side, has described it in these terms:-"Next to the bay of Naples, I know of no place more capable of being converted into a finished picture by the hand of art and industry than this. Let only the immense forest on the slope give place to cultivated plantations of sugar-canes,—the brow of the hill be studded with coffee-trees,—and a town of sufficient importance to form the cap. of the island be built on the rising ground near the E angle of the bay, where a river, navigable for boats drawing 7 or 8 ft. of water, would flow beneath it,—and F. would far surpass any of the islands of the British possessions in the W. Indies." To this spot, marked in our chart (d), Capt. Kelly gave the name of George's bay.—The island, it will be seen from the above sketch, has a quadrilateral outline. The capes, at its four extreme angles, are Cape Bullen, on the NW; Cape Horatio, on the NE; Cape Barrow, on the SE; and an unnamed point on the SW .- A small islet on the E side (e) is called Leven island; and an islet to the N of George bay <math>(f)</math>, Goat island.
FERNELL, or FARNELL, a parish of Forfarshire, 3 m. in length, and 2 m. in breadth, stretching along the S bank of the S. Esk, 3 m. SW of Brechin. Pop.
FERNESS, a cape on the W coast of Eday, one of the Orkney islands, in N lat. 59° 2'.

FERNEY, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Ain, and 6 m. SE of Gex, on the frontier of Switzerland, 5 m. NNW of Geneva. Pop. in 1841, 1,214. In 1762, the adjacent lands were purchased by Voltaire, who wished to raise the v. to a town. With this view he attracted to it a number of workmen and artists, particularly watchmakers from Geneva, erected a church, and made a variety of improvements. In the course of 15 years, the pop. was carried to 1,200; but in 1786, 8 years after Voltaire's death, the number did not exceed 600, and has not since increased. The chateau occupied by Voltaire is preserved nearly in the same state, and is still shown to strangers.—The cant., comprising 9 com., had a pop. of 5,225 in 1841.

FERNS, a parish and town in co. Wexford. Area of the p., 10,411 acres. Pop. in 1841, 2,860. The surface consists, for the most part, of good arable land; and is drained SW to the Slaney by the Bann rivulet. The N division terminates at the summit of Slievebog, whose alt. above sea-level is 1,385 ft. The decayed and ancient town of F., the seat of a diocese, stands on the road from Dublin to Wexford, 53 m. N by E of Enniscorthy, and 561 m. S by W of Dublin, a brief distance NW of the Bann or Banna rivulet, on the side of a hill whose summit bears aloft the conspicuous ruins of an old castle. With the exception of these ruins, and of the episcopal palace and cathedral, F. now consists of little else than a few small, neatly-thatched cabins, and is in every practical sense a mere village, even scarcely more than a hamlet. Its pop. in 1841 was only 632. The present cathedral is a modern building, attached to some remains of a more ancient structure. The original rude castle or humble palace of the kings of Leinster, occupied the same site as the present ruins; and seems to have been swept away to make room for a regular fortalice by Earl Strongbow. During the war of 1641, the castle and town fell under the power of the parliamentarian general, Sir C. Coote; when the former was permanently dismantled, and the latter made the scene of tragical bloodshed, and irretrievably precipitated from its rank of importance among the towns of Ireland.—The bishopric of F. affects to have been founded in the last quarter of the 6th cent. The gross and the net episcopal revenue upon an average of 3 years ending in 1831, was respectively £3,882 and £3,231. The dio. comprehends the whole of the co. of Wexford, and 2 ps. in the co. of Wicklow. Its length from N to S is about 46 m.; its breadth is 18 m.; and its area is 576,471 acres. Pop. in 1831, 190,230. Total of parishes, 138; of benefices, 58; of single parishes constituting benefices, 26. Total of churches, 61; of sittings, 15,917. Total of Protestant dissenting places of worship, including 1 Presbyterian, 15; of Roman Catholic chapels, 91. In 1834 the pop. consisted of 24,672 Churchmen, 19 Presbyterians, 300 other Protestant dissenters, and 172,789 Roman Catholics.—The Roman Catholic diocese of F. continues unannexed; and in 1838 was distributed into 35 parishes, and had 33 parochial clergy, and 46 curates or coadjutors.
FEROKABAD, a town of Hindostan, on the coast of Malabar, 10 m. S of Calicut.
FEROLETO, a village of Naples, in the Basilicata, 18 m. S of Matera. It is a poor place, built of unbaked bricks laid on rows of broken tiles. Pop. 600. In the vicinity are sulphureous springs.
FERON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Trelon, 6 m. SE of Avesnes. Pop. 589.

FEROZABAD, a town of Bengal, in the prov. and 24 m. E of Agra. It is walled, and of some commercial importance.—Also a town in the Ni- FER ×04 01 zam's dominions, on the Bima, 110 m. WSW of Hyderabad.

FEROZEPUR, or FIRUZPUR, a town of India, in the prov. of Agra, and principality of Hacheri, in N lat. 27° 58', E long. 76° 39'.—Also a town of Sirhind, 3 m. from the Sutledge, 60 m. direct distance W of Ludiana, 950 m. from the mouth of the Indus. It contains a commanding castle, and has recently been surrounded by a wall and ditch, so as to secure it from any predatory attack. Good bazaars have been laid out, and the town is rapidly filling with settlers under the British protection. From this town there are easy roads into all parts of the protected Sikh states; and it is only 14 marches or 169 m. to Bahr, in the valley of Punjore, which is the place to which all European supplies for Simlah are sent. On the opposite or r. bank of the Sutledge are direct roads to Umritsir and Lahore.—This town has given name to one of the most important battles ever fought on the continent of India. On the 21st of November 1845, the Sikh army, in great strength, and with a vast field of artillery, moved from Lahore, and advancing by short marches towards the British frontier, on the 11th of December began to cross the Sutledge. The l. bank of that river being at the moment undefended, the Sikhs proceeded to construct an immense fortified camp at the v. of Ferozeshah, 10 m. ESE of Ferozepur. This camp was a parallelogram 1 m. in length, and a 1 m. in breadth, and enclosed the v., which was also fortified: it had above 100 guns in position in front, and a deep ditch was drawn round the whole. Sir John Littler was at this moment stationed at Ferozepur, but with a force totally inadequate to resist the enemy, who began to threaten that place, but were met at Mudki by a small force which had been hastily pushed forward from Umbala and Ludiana, and defeated with great slaughter, and the loss of 17 pieces of artillery. This victory enabled the commander-in-chief to open a communication with Sir John Littler in F., and an arrangement was entered into with him for a simultaneous attack on the enemy's intrenchments in the neighbourhood. The enemy, believed to be 50,000 strong, occupied a most advantageous position, flanked by wood and jungle, and industriously fortified by art. The governor-general's army fell short of 17,000 men, and was at once deficient in artillery and in ammunition. During the whole of the 21st and 22d of December, the fate of India hung suspended upon the edge of battle. Towards the dusk of evening of the 21st, the British forces penetrated into the enemy's camp, which throughout a large portion of its extent was in flames. Having carried the enemy's intrenched camp, our generals supposed that their task was accomplished, and that the Sikhs were in full retreat towards the Sutledge; but at this moment a fresh alarm was spread, and a large body of Sikhs, estimated at nearly 30,000, came pouring into the field, with an immense reserve of horse, and a formidable field of artillery. This army is supposed to have been encamped somewhere in the vicinity of F., where its existence remained wholly unknown. The battle was now renewed under circumstances the most unfavourable possible for the British arms: thrice was the assault made, and as many times was the enemy repulsed. The intrenched camp of the Sikhs was again carried; but not being able to follow up our victory, they were left nearly as much masters of the country as before. On retreating from their camp they marched S, in the direction of F., which, had they been so disposed, they might at that moment perhaps have taken. The battle of Aliwal, however, completed the destruction of Runjit Singh's army.
FERRAGAN, or FARRAGON, a mountain in the co. of Perth, p. of Dull, 18 m. S of Blair-Athol. Alt. 2,535 ft.
FERRAH. See FURRAH.

FERRAJO (PORTO), a town on the island of Elba, 5 m. WSW of Cape Vita, which, from its size and neatness, may be regarded as the cap. Its streets are formed upon terraces cut out of the rock; the houses are handsome; and the pop. is about 5,000. The harbour is capable of receiving ships-of-the-line. It is commanded by two lofty forts, and has a lighthouse in N lat. 42° 49′ 5″, E log. 10° 20′ 20″.
FERRALS-LES-MONTAGE ES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant. of Olonzac, 8 m. SW of Saint-Pons. Pop. 1,272.
FERRANDINA, a small town of Naples, in the Basilicata, on the Bassenta, 15 m. SW of Matera.

FERRARA, a duchy and legation in the E part Pop. 5,200. of Upper Italy, forming the most northern part of the Papal domains. It is nearly of a triangular shape; one side stretching 25 m. along the shores of the Adriatic on the E; another about 58 m. to the N; the third runs about 67 m. W and S, skirting the duchies of Mantua and Modena, also the provs. of Bologna and Ravenna, and the Venetian states. The superficial area has been estimated at 2,736,093 tavole quadrate censuale, or 826 sq. m. of 60 to a degree. The surface is in general level E from the river Po, which is here navigable, and divides into two branches, called Po-di-Volano and Po-di-Primaro, both flowing into the Adriatic, and each having a harbour at its mouth. Besides these, it is fertilized by the streams Panaro, Reno, Sillaro, Santerno, Senio, and Lamone, the last of which streams divides it from the leg. of Ravenna, and by numerous artificial canals.—This duchy contains three cities, Ferrara, the cap., Commacchio, and Cento. Besides these there are 18 towns with their own peculiar and exclusive jurisdiction, and 162 parochial vs. In 1784 the total pop. of the F. territory amounted to 235,234; in 1833 it was returned at only 210,883; in 1843 it amounted to 218,786.—The principal products of the duchy consist in grain, fruit, wine, flax and hemp, silk, and wool. The soil is marshy. The land is generally divided into large properties, and the greater part is as badly cultivated as it is thinly peopled. Some revenue arises from imports and exports, from the fisheries of Commacchio which are very profitable, from salt-works, and other sources; but there cannot be much trade in a territory of such extent, when an annual fair in one of the small towns is considered a remarkable event. F. is governed by a cardinal, who is sent to the chief city every three years as legate a latere; a dignified prelate, with the title of vice-legate, rules in his absence. The detail of civil and criminal matters is committed to the cognizance of certain judges appointed by the Pope; and there is a senate or grand-council of 100 persons chosen from the nobles, merchants, and citizens, and renewed every three years, from which 10 persons of each order are selected to constitute an annual magistracy. In respect to its ecclesiastical state, the duchy is divided into 9 dioceses, at the head of which is an archbishop, generally a cardinal, endowed with ample revenues, and possessing a jurisdiction over 135 ps. There are 60 monasteries of different orders, containing 1,350 monks and priests; 10 convents, containing about 620 nuns; and 3 hospitals, in which 50 boys are maintained. The legation is subdivided into the two distretti or districts of F. and Lugo. The former contains the towns of F., Argenta, Bondero, Cento, Codigoro, Commacchio, Copparo, and Porto-Maggiore; the latter, those of Lugno, Bagna-Cavallo, and Massa-Lomborda.—The duchy of F. is said to have been recognised in very FER ancient times as an important territory. It was a sovereign and independent state under the dominion of its own dukes of the family of Este, who began to govern here in 1205. On the death of Alphonso II., in 1597, Pope Clement VIII., on the plea of the illegitimacy of Cæsar d'Este, declared the duchy had devolved to the Papal see, and took possession of it in person. Along with the other Italian provinces it became implicated in the consequences of the French revolution. It was ceded by the Roman pontiff in 1797 to the government of France, and by it constituted the department of the Lower Po; but on the restitution of the Pope, it again became an integral part of the territories of the apostolic chamber.

FERRARA, the cap. of the duchy of F., is situated on the N bank of the Po, which here divides into two branches, called Po-di-Volano and Po-di-Primaro, both flowing to the E, in N lat. 44° 49', E long. 11° 41', 25 m. NE of Bologna. It is surrounded by a fortified wall, and a broad ditch which may be filled with water by means of a canal from the river. It has 5 gates, and at the SW extremity there is a regular fortress. Within the walls are some gardens which enlarge the dimensions ascribed to the city. There are several squares, and the streets are wide and in many instances regularly and superbly built. The suburbs of St. Luke and St. George are without the walls, on the opposite side of the river, which is crossed by bridges near two of the gates. The principal objects in the city are churches, convents, a few edifices for public purposes not ecclesiastical, and a few superb mansions belonging to private individuals, some of which are, however, empty and abandoned. The Duomo or metropolitan church occupies one side of the Piazza-di-San-Crispino, the principal square. The antiquity of this edifice remounts to 1135, when it was consecrated; but the part beyond the transept was modernized in 1637, and the rest of the church between 1712 and 1735. Pope Urban having died in F. in 1187, was interred here, and his successor Gregory was elected in the church. The architecture of the tower, built in 1412, is much celebrated. About the year 1506, a spacious edifice, the church of St. Benedict, was built by two native architects in the W part of the city, to which a monastery adjoins. Here were deposited the remains of the poet Ariosto, which were subsequently transferred to the Lyceum, and his house is still shown to strangers. The church dedicated to St. Francis, founded at an early period, frequently renewed and altered, and completed in 1495, is rich in pictures and statues, and presents several beautiful sculptures of different descriptions. It is remarkable for an echo which repeats the voice of a person standing in a particlar position, or the slightest footstep near the main entrance, 15 or 16 times distinctly, and afterwards more faintly, for some intervals. The church of St. Dominic, a spacious modern edifice, with a monastery of Dominican monks connected with it, stands in the SW part of the city. It was founded in 1710, and completed in 1726. The archbishop's palace adjacent to the metropolitan church, a modern building, has some statues, and a staircase which is much admired. Not far from the last of these buildings stands a vast and superb Gothic edifice, with towers at each angle, and ornamented by a balustrade, called the Castle, and occupied by the cardinal-legate. A military guard is mounted here, and the grand council assembles here in a large hall devoted to that purpose. The hospital of St. Anne presents much the same aspect it might have done in 1579 when Tasso was condemned to its solitary cell. There are two foundling hospitals for male and female children, one for orphans, and a poor's house for mendicants; also different public buildings for the administration of justice, one of which, called the palace of reason, was erected in the year 1326. The theatre is a modern structure of Ionic architecture. F. is said to have been early distinguished in the annals of literature, and the foundation of a university here was authorised in 1391 by Pope Leo XII. The seminary which now exists, however, is of more modern date, having either been built or altered in 1610. The building itself is a plain structure, with a handsome gateway, surmounted by a tower and cupola. There are 22 chairs embracing the principal branches of science, an anatomical theatre, a school of the fine arts, a museum of antiquities, and a botanical garden. It was recently attended by about 300 students. In sculptures, cameos, coins, inscriptions, and mosaic work, the museum of F. is said to be considerable; but it has most probably shared in the dilapidations which have diminished the curiosities of Italy. The library of the university is of more recent institution, having been commenced by the purchase of that of Cardinal Bentivoglio, in 1743; the library of the college of the Jesuits was added some time after, and it was opened for public use in 1750. This library is now said to comprise 80,000 vols. Among the manuscripts are some of Ariosto's poems, written in his own hand.—The pop. of F. in 1824 was 24,000; in 1784 it was 31,250. The modern city dates its foundation from the 5th century, when the invasion of the Huns, and the destruction of Aquileia, drove the inhabitants to take refuge amid marshes and forests. In 585, it was strengthened by Smaragdus, exarch of Ravenna; but the era of its glory dates from the 13th cent., under the house of Este, when "the little courts of F. and Urbino seemed to emulate those of Alexandria and Pergamos, contending for pre-eminence only in literature and elegance." [Forsyth.]
FERRATO (CAPE), a rugged white point, 70 or 80 ft. in height, on the E coast of Sardinia, in N lat. 39° 19′, E long. 9° 39′ 40″.
FERRAZANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Molise, 1 m. SSE of Campobasso. Pop. 2,450.
FERRE' (CAPE), a cape on the SE coast of Martinico, in N lat. 14° 27′ 39′, W long. 60° 46′ 0″.
FERRE' (LA), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Louvigné-du- Desert, 10 m. N of Fougeres. Pop. 1,777.
FERREANAH. See FERIANA.

FERREIRA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. and in the immediate vicinity of Goyaz, at the confluence of a river of the same name with the Vermelho.— Also an island near the coast of the district of Parati, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro.—Also an island in the bay of Nitheroi, opposite São-Christovão.
FERREIRA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo. It is small, but fortified, and stands on a hill near the I. bank of the Safrins, 13 m. W of Beja.
FERREIRA-DE-AVES, a small town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 15 m. NE of Viseu.
FERREIRA'S RIVER, a stream of S. Africa, flowing into Algoa bay, 4 m. NE of Markham's Cove, and 2 m. SW by W of the mouth of the Zwartkops. It is closed by a bank of sand except at spring-tides.

FERRE'OL-D'AUROURE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Loire, cant. of Saint-Didier-la-Seauve, 18 m. NNE of Yssingeaux. Pop. 1,388. It has a mine of lead; and some papermills are established here.
FERRER COVE, a deep inlet on the W side of Nootka island, in N lat. 49° 44'.
FERRERS BAY, a bay on the N coast of Egmont island, or New Guernsey, 5 m. E of Carteret's point.
FERRET (CAPE), a point of land on the W FER coast of France, in the bay of Arcachon. See ARCACHON.
FERRET ISLAND, a small island near the E coast of Labrador, in N lat. 53° 40'.

FERRET (COL), a pass of the Pennine Alps, on the confines of the Swiss cant. of Valais and of the Sardinian division of Aosta, 6 m. W of the great St. Bernard, and 12 m. NE of Mont Blanc, at the NE extremity of the valley of the same name, which separates the great chain of Mont Blanc from the mountains of which Mont Velan forms the culminating point. It rises to the height of 7,740 ft. above sea-level; and is traversed by the road from Martigny to Courmayeur.

FERRETTE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Haut-Rhin, arrond. of Altkirch. The cant. comprises 31 com. Pop. in 1831, 15,325; in 1841, 15,913.—The town is 11 m. SSE of Altkirch, on an affluent of the Ill. Pop. 792. It has an old castle. Fairs are held here 8 times a-year.
FERREYK, or FAREGG (WADY), a valley and caravan station in Nubia, in the Wady Nuba, on the r. bank of the Nile, 30 m. SW of Derr.
FERREYRA, or FERREIRA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 36 m. NNW of Almeria, partido and 11 m. SE of Guadix.
FERRIBY (NORTH), a village and parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. WSW of Hull, with a station on the Hull and Selby railway. Area 3,760 acres. Pop. 935.
FERRIBY (South), a parish in Lincolnshire, on the Humber, 3 m. WSW of Barton. Area 1,750 acres. Pop. 542.

FERRIERE, a commune and village of the duchy of Parma, in the district of Plaisance. It has extensive iron and copper founderies.—Also a commune of Belgium, in the prov. and 23 m. S of Liege, on the Ourthe. Pop. 1,005.—Also a commune in the prov. of Brabant, cant. of Aroux-Mousty. Pop. 55.—Also a commune in the prov. of Liege, cant. of Lavoir. Pop. 56.

FERRIERE (LA), or FERRARA, a hamlet in the prov. and 7 m. NNW of Susa, in a valley of extreme wildness, on the Cenise, which is here crossed by a wooden bridge, and on the old road from Mont Cenis by the Novalaise. Pop. 159.

FERRIERE (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Doubs, com. of Jougne, 11 m. S of Pontarlier. Pop. 108. Scythes are extensively manufactured here.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Isere, cant. of Allevard, 21 m. NE of Grenoble, on the r. bank of the Ozeins. Pop. 1,201.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Vendee, cant. of Les- Essarts, 8 m. from Bourbon-Vendee. Pop. 1,510. Coal and porcelain clay are found in this locality.— Also a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne and amt of Courtlari. Pop. 1,260.
FERRIERE-AUX-ETANGS (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Messey, 9 m. NE of Domfront. Pop. 1,448. It has extensive glass-works.

FERRIERE - LA - GRANDE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Maubeuge. Pop. 1,021. It has extensive quarries of marble and of freestone, an iron-mine and forge, and blast furnaces, and a lime-kiln.
FERRIERE - SAINT - HILAIRE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Eure, cant. of Broglie. Pop. 599. It has an iron-foundry.
FERRIERE-LA-PETITE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Maubeuge. Pop. 700. It has quarries of marble and granite, and several manufactories of earthenware.

FERRIERES, a commune of France, in the dep. * of the Allier, cant. of Maget-la-Montagne, on the Sichon, 4 m. from La Pelisse. Pop. in 1841, 3,228. It has a quarry of fine blue marble.—Also a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Loiret, arrond. of Montarges. The cant. comprises 17 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,037; in 1841, 10,605. The town is 8 m. NNE of Montargis, on the Bied, and near an extensive marsh. Pop. 1,708. It contains the ruins of an abbey, of which the foundation is attributed to Clovis.-Also a village in the dep. of Correze, cant. and 6 m. S of Larche.

FERRISBURG, a township of Addison co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., 19 m. S of Burlington, drained by Otter, Little Otter, and Lewis creeks, and bounded on the W by Lake Champlain. It is hilly in some parts, but very fertile. It has a good harbour on lake Champlain. Pop. in 1840, 1,755.

FERRO, or HIERRO, the most westerly of the Canary islands, intersected by the meridian of 18° W, and the parallel of 27° 40' N. From its extreme NE point, called Norte point (a), in N lat. 27° 50' 20", to its extreme S point, called Restinga point (b), in N lat. 27° 37′ 35″, its length from NE by N to SW by S is 15 m.; from Restinga point, to Dehesa point (c), it measures in a NW by N direction 125 m. Its cap., Valverde (V), is situated among a group of mountains on the NE coast, whose seaport is Port Hierro (H), in N lat. 27° 46′ 30″, W long. 17° 54' 12". F. contains about 100 sq. m., and a pop. of 4,337. Besides being the smallest of the Canaries, F. is the most arid and barren. It is a high mountain-mass composed of dark grey lava, sprinkled here and there with low sombre bushes, and appearing from sea to be traversed by white chalk-like stripes. Its highest summits are San- Anton-del-Pinal (P), 3,907 ft.; a peak to the NNE (d), 4,990 ft.; and one to the W (e), 4,580 ft. It presents on all sides to the sea a face of bold and craggy rocks; but in the interior the appearance of the country improves; and a considerable part is tolerably level and fruitful. Good wine and brandy are made, and a considerable portion of both exported to Teneriffe. Bees thrive well on account of the multitude of aromatic flowers with which the ground is covered, and the honey is excellent. F. abounds particularly in figs, which are dried and exported; the quantity is sometimes so great, that to prevent their being lost it is necessary to convert them into brandy. F. suffers great disadvantage from the scarcity of water. The cattle are said sometimes to quench their thirst with sea-water. F., being once supposed to be the most W point of the old world, was originally employed by geographers as their first meridian, and the longitude reckoned from it. * FER
FERRO (CAPE), a promontory on the E side of the gulf of Stora, in Algeria, in N lat. 37° 7′ 10", E long. 7° 17′ 15″.

FERROL (EL), an important seaport of Spain, in the prov. and 12 m. NE of Corunna, on the N coast of Galicia, at the influx of the river Juvia into the extensive inlet called the bay of Corunna or Betanzos, in N lat. 43° 29', W long. 8° 12' [Raper]. The town is of recent erection, having been only a v. until 1752, when Ensennada, minister of Ferdinand VI., apprised of the real advantages of its situation, determined to establish dockyards, arsenals, and manufactories here. The new town is regularly built, with streets crossing each other at right angles, and two plazas. The harbour, for depth, capacity, and safety, is not equalled by many in Europe, though it is not easy to clear out of it with every wind. Its position renders it extremely strong; for to approach it from the sea, it is necessary that vessels advance, one by one, in a narrow channel 2 m. in length, with a depth of from 8 to 12 fath., commanded by forts, and which may even be shut by a stoccado; while, on the land-side, it is protected by its distance from any point of landing, and the strength of its fortifications, which consist of redoubts with 4 or 5 guns on each face, and 3 or 4 on each flank, joined together by an intrenchment, and a breast-work which masks the works in the interior. The tide in the channel leading to the harbour runs so strongly that it is advisable to enter or leave before high and low water. The basin in which the ships are laid up is of great extent and solid workmanship; it is nearly ½ a m. in length, and a ¼ m. in breadth; and each ship has its own store-house, where the boatswains, carpenters, and gunners' stores, are distinctly marked. The marine barracks are a vast and beautiful building affording accommodation for 6,000 men. The establishments here are all naval. There is an academy for the guardas marinas; a mathematical school for marine artillerists: a nautical and a pilot school. F. is not a place of trade, the harbour being frequented only by coasting-barks; and the manufactures confined to sail-cloths, ropes, hardware, hats, paper, and leather. The pop. in 1826 was 13,384; in 1845, 15,720. The climate is moist from the vicinity of the Atlantic.
FERROL BAY, an inlet on the coast of Peru, 3 leagues from Samanco bay, from which it is separated by a low sandy isthmus, in S lat. 9° 06' 30", W long. 78° 39′ 25″.
FERROLLE ISLAND, an islet on the W coast of Newfoundland, N of Beach-point, in N lat. 51° 6'.
FERROLLE (POINT), the termination of a peninsular projection on the W coast of Newfoundland, in N lat. 51° 2', W long. 57° 5'.

FERRYBRIDGE, a neat village in the p. of Frystone-Ferry, W. R. of York, 2 m. NE of Pontefract, on the river Aire, which is here crossed by a bridge, and near the junction of the North Midland railway with the Leeds and Selby line.—An obstinate battle was fought here between the adherents of the houses of York and Lancaster.
FERRYDEN, a fishing-village of Scotland, in the co. of Forfar, p. of Craig, on the S bank of the S. Esk, 1 m. S of Montrose. Pop. 921.

FERRYHILL, a township in the p. of Merrington, co. of Durham, at the terminus of the Byers' Green branch of the Clarence railway, and on the line of the York, Newcastle, and Berwick railway, by which it is 10 m. S of Durham. Pop. 850. There are coal-works in the vicinity.
FERRYLAND HARBOUR, a bay on the E coast of Newfoundland, in N lat. 47° 8'.

FERRY-PORT-ON-CRAIG, a village and parish of Scotland, in the NE of Fife, at the mouth of the river Tay. Area of p. 2,026 Scots acres. Pop. in 1801, 920; in 1841, 1,714. A huge breastwork or mole has recently been erected here, with a landingslip, in connection with the Edinburgh, Perth, and Dundee railway, to facilitate the embarking and disembarking loaded trains between this point and the Forfarshire coast at Broughty-ferry.
FERSFIELD, a parish in Norfolk, 4 m. WNW of Diss. Area 1,390 acres. Pop. 295.
FERSINA, a river of Tyrol, which runs into the Adige 2 m. N of Trent.

FERTE' (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ardennes, cant. of Carignan. Pop. 456. It has manufactories of cloth, and wool spinning-mills. -It may be noticed here, with reference to the immediately following articles, that the term ferte signifies in Old French, 'a feudal fortress.'

FERTE'-ALEPS (LA), or LA FERTE'-ALAIS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Oise, arrond. of Etampes.—The cant. comprises 18 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,205; in 1841, 8,998.—The town is 11 m. ENE of Etampes, on the r. bank of the Essonne. Pop. 875. It has extensive silk and cotton spinning-mills, and several manufactories of cloth. Freestone is quarried in the environs. The rearing of bees forms an important branch of local industry.

FERTE'-BERNARD (LA), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, arrond. of Mamers.—The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,104; in 1841, 13,806.—The town is 20 m. SE of Mamers, on the l. bank of the Huine. Pop. 2,583. It is enclosed by an old wall, and has an ancient castle in a good state of preservation, now used as a town-house; a handsome parish church, a public library, and an alms-house. It has extensive manufactories of cloth for export to the colonies, giving employment to upwards of 1,000 persons in the locality; numerous wool spinning, tan, and fulling-mills; and several bleacheries, tanneries, and tilekilns. Fairs are held 7 times a-year.

FERTE'-FRESNEL (LA), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Orne, arrond. of Argentan.—The cant. comprises 19 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,969; in 1841, 8,972.—The town is 30 m. ENE of Argentan. Pop. 479. It has a fine castle.

FERTE'-GAUCHER (LA), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et- Marne, arrond. of Coulommiers.—The cant. comprises 19 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,529; in 1841, 12,005. The town is 12 m. E of Coulommiers, in a narrow valley, on the Grand-Morin. Pop. 2,009. It has an hospital; and possesses considerable manufactories of serge, several paper and tan mills, and numerous lime, tile, and brick kilns. The trade consists chiefly in grain and flour .- A sanguinary battle took place here in 1814 between the Allies and the French.
FERTE'-IMBAULT (LA), a town of France, in the dep. of the Loir-et-Cher, cant. of Salbris, 11 m. E of Romorantin, between the Sandre and Coussin. Pop. 1,600.
FERTE'-LANGERON (LA), a village of France, in the dep. of the Nievre, cant. and 5 m. SSE of Saint-Pierre-le-Moutier, near the r. bank of the Loire. Pop. 1,100.
FERTE'-LOUPTIERE (LA), a town of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, cant. of Charny, 11 m. SW of Ivigny. Pop. 1,100.

FERTE'-MACE' (LA), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Orne, arrond. of Domfront.—The cant. comprises 8 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,367; in 1841, 15,831.—The town is 14 m. E of Domfront. Pop. 5,197. It has extensive manufactories of ticking, coarse cotton cloth, ginghams, FER calico, tape, wooden snuff-boxes and combs, and parchment; besides several distilleries of brandy, numerous tanneries, tile-kilns, and dye-works, and excellent bleacheries. Honey and lint are extensively cultivated in the environs, and, with spices and dye-stuffs, form the chief articles of local trade. Fairs are held twice a-year.

FERTE'-MILO'N (LA), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Aisne, cant. of Neuillysur-Front, on the Ourcq, near the forest of Villers- Colèrets. Pop. 1,944. It is enclosed by a wall, and was formerly defended by a fortress, the ruins of which only now remain. It has 3 churches and an hospital, and possesses several tanneries and bleacheries. Grain and timber are sent hence in large quantities to Paris. Fairs are held four times a-year. This town is noted as the birth-place of Jean- Racine.

FERTE'-SUR-AMANCE (LA), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Haute- Marne, arrond. of Langres.—The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 6,621; in 1841, 6,768.—The town is near the l. bank of the Amance, 21 m. E of Langres. Pop. 628. Fairs are held here four times a-year.

FERTE'-SUR-AUBE (LA), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Marne, cant. of Château-Vilain, on the l. bank of the Aube. Pop. 1,131. It has a blast furnace and forge, and is an extensive depot of fire-wood for the supply of Paris,

FERTE'-SAINT-AUBIN (LA), or LA FERTE' SENNETERRE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Loiret, arrond. of Orleans.—The cant. comprises 7 com. Pop. in 1831, 5,289; in 1841, 5,650.—The town is on the l. bank of the Cosson. Pop. 2,233. It has 4 annual fairs.

FERTE'-SUR-GRONE (LA), a village of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Senecyle-Grand, at the confluence of the Grône and Grison, 8 m. S of Châlons-sur-Saône. Pop. 450. It has a manufactory of sewing-cotton.

FERTE'-SOUS-JOUARRE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et- Marne, arrond. of Meaux.—The cant. comprises 19 com. Pop. in 1831, 16,240; in 1841, 16,378.—The town is 12 m. E of Meaux, on an island formed by the Marne, and a little above the confluence of the Petit-Morin. Pop. 4,105. The chateau de La Barre, and an alms-house, are the only buildings which it contains worthy of note; but its environs are fertile and well-cultivated, and are adorned with numerous villas. This town is the entrepot of an extensive trade in mill-stone, quarried in the locality, and of wool, corn, wood, and charcoal, for the Paris market. Fairs are held four times a-year.—F. is noted as the birth-place of Cardinal de Bourbon, legate of Avignon.
FERTE'-SUR-PE'RON (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aisne, cant. of Ribemont. Pop. 1,244,
FERTE'-SOUPIE'RE (LA), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, cant. of Charny, 11 m. SW of Ivigny. Pop. 1,308. It has an old castle.

FERTE'-VIDAME (LA), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Eure-et-Loir, arrond. of Dreux. The cant. comprises 8 com. Pop. in 1831, 3,262; in 1841, 3,219. The village is 25 m. WSW of Dreux, Pop. 772. It has two annual fairs.
FERTE'-VILLENEUILLE (LA), a town of France, in the dep. of the Eure-et-Loir, cant. and 5 m. ESE of Cloye, on the Aigre. Pop. 400.
FERTIT, a district of Eastern Sudan, to the S of Darfur. It contains extensive copper-mines. Its inhabitants are Pagans, and speak a peculiar dialect.
FERTRUE, or FORTELBACH, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Haut-Rhin, com. of Sainte-Marieaux-Mines. Pop. 1,131.
FERVACQUES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Livarot, on the Toucques. Pop. 1,146. It has manufactories of coarse woollen fabrics and several tanneries.
FERWERD, a village of Holland, in the prov. of Friesland, cant. and 3 m. NNE of Hallum, and 10 m. N of Leuwarden. Pop. 1,100.

FERZAH, a district and village of Afghanistan, in the Koh-i-Damun, on the E side of the Pughman mountains. The village lies on a small stream of the same name, an affluent of the Punchshir, 30 m. NW of Cabul. The district is extremely picturesque and highly productive, especially in fruit. It comprises 12 villages and 4 forts, and a pop. of about 4,000, chiefly of Afghans and Tajiks.
FESHIE, a stream of Inverness-shire, which, rising near Cairneilar, flows N into the Spey near Alvie.
FESHKAH (RAS-EL), a point of land near the NW extremity of the Dead sea.
FESLE, a town of Haiti, on the S coast, in the dep. of the West, 23 m. SSE of Port-au-Prince, and 27 m. E of Jacmel.

FESSA (WADY), a river of Barbary, which takes its rise in the Jebel Fissat, near the SE confines of Tunis, runs ENE into Tripoli, and falls into the gulf of Al-Biban a little to the N of Kela, after a course of about 60 m.
FESSENHEIM, a village of France, in the dep. of the Haut-Rhin, SE of Colmar. Pop. 826.
FESSY, a village of Savoy, in the prov. of Chablais, 8 m. S of Thonon. Pop. 388.
FESTALEMS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Dordogne, cant. of Saint-Aulaye. Pop. 1,068.

FESTENBERG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia, capital of a seignory, regency and 10 m. NE of Breslau and circle of Wartenberg. Pop. 2,476. It has a castle and 2 churches, and possesses some manufactories of cloth.
FESTINIOG, a parish and hamlet in Merionethshire, 5 m. N of Trawsfynydd. Pop. 3,138. The hamlet is 3 m. NE of Tan-y-Bwlch.
FESTUBERT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Cambrin, 5 m. E of Bethune. Pop. 1,542. It has extensive manufactories of linen and several bleacheries.
FETEHAM, a parish in Surrey, 14 m. SW of Leatherhead, and W of the Mole. Area 1,750 acres. Pop. 373.
FETH-ISLAM. See GLADOVA.

FETHARD, a municipal town and parish of co. Tipperary, 8 m. SE of Cashel. Area of p., 1,530 acres. Pop. 4,410.—Also a parish and village in co. Wexford, 31 m. SE of Duncannon. Area of p., 3,930 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,772. Area of v., 9 acres. Pop. 406.
FETIO, a cape and port of Greece, in the diocese of Phthiotis, to the W of the channel of Trikeri, in N lat. 39° 3′, and E long. 22° 57′.

FETLAR, one of the most northerly of the Shetland isles, between 60° 34' and 60° 38' N lat., and intersected by the meridian of 0° 52' W, separated from North Yell on the W, and from Unst on the N, by a sound 2 m. in breadth at its narrowest part. The island is 5½ m. long from NW to SE, and 2½ m. in average breadth, comprising an area of 12,000 acres. Pop. in 1831, 843; in 1841, 761.—Its principal bays are Tresta, Aith, Funzie, Gruting-voe, Urie, Sand, and Mowick.
FETO (POINT), a headland of Sicily, on the SW coast, in the prov. of Trapani, district and 3 m. FEU WNW of Mazzara, in N lat. 30° 39', and E long. 12° 30′.
FETTAN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, and district of Unter Engadin, on the l. bank of the Inn. Pop. 540.
FETTERANGUS, a village in the p. of Old Deer, Banffshire. Pop. 225.

FETTERCAIRN, a parish and village of Kincardineshire, separated from Forfarshire on the W by the North Esk, and at the foot of the NE range of the Grampians. Area 14.359 English acres. Pop. 1,793. Pop. of village, 280.
FETTERESSO, a parish in Kincardineshire, extending NW from the sea, and comprising an area of 24,914 sq. acres. Pop. 5,212.
FETU, a town of Upper Guinea, in the district of Aguafu, 5 m. NW of Cape Coast Castle.
FETUGA, or HOOD ISLAND, an island of the South Pacific, in the Mindanao archipelago, to the NE of Ohivaoa, or Dominica island, in S lat. 9° 20', and W long. 138° 30'.
FEUCHT, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Middle-Franconia, presidial of Altdorf, 8 m. SE of Nürnberg. Pop. 562.

FEUCHTWANG, a landgericht and town of Bavaria, in the circle of Middle-Franconia, 16 m. SW of Anspach, on the Sulz. Pop. 2,530. It is enclosed by walls and ditches, and contains 3 churches and an hospital. It has extensive manufactories of cloth, woollen damask and muslin, and several tanneries.
FEUERBACH, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Neckar, bail. and 2 m. NW of Stuttgard. Pop. in 1840, 2,584.
FEUERTHALEN, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Zurich, bail. and 8 m. NNW of Andelfingen, on the L bank of the Rhine, opposite Schaffhausen. Pop. 665.
FEUGAROLLES, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Loire, cant. and com. of Le Chambon. Pop. 2,015.
FEUGAROLLES, or FUGAROLLES-D'ALBRET, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Lavardac, and 8 m. N of Nerac. Pop. 1,292.
FEUGH, a rivulet in Kincardineshire, which rises in Aberdeenshire, and after receiving the Avon and Dye, falls into the Dee.
FEUILLADE, a village of France, in the dep. of the Charente, cant. and 5 m. SSW of Montbron, on the Bandiat. Pop. 707. In the vicinity are several iron-mines.

FEUILLE'E (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Finistere, cant. of Huelgoat, 32 m. NNE of Quimper, on a hill. Pop. 1,782. It has a chapel, which was originally a pagan temple.—Also a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine- Inferieure, cant. of Argeuil, 22 m. S of Neuchâtelen-Bray. Pop. 2,154.

FEUQUIE'RES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Somme, cant. of Moyenville. Pop. 1,322.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Oise, cant. of Grandvilliers, 21 m. NNW of Beauvais. Pop. 1,329. Hosiery is manufactured here.

FEURS, a canton, commune, and town of France in the dep. of the Loire, arrond. of Montbrison.—The cant. comprises 18 com. Pop. in 1831, 16,964; in 1841, 18,348.—The town, 14 m. NE of Montbrison, is situated in a marshy but fertile plain, near the r. bank of the Loire. Pop. 2,816. It has a small port, and a considerable trade in articles of local produce. Fairs are held six times a-year.—This town is of great antiquity. It occupies the site of the Forum Segusianorum of the Romans, considerable remains of which are still to be found in the locality. Till 1441 it was the capital of the prov. of Forez. In 1562 it was clined in importance.
FEVE, a river of the state of Arkansas, U.S., which has its source about 210 m. SW of Cadron, and falls into the Arkansas, after a course of about 240 m.
FEVERSHAM. See FAVERSHAM.
FEWCOTT, a hamlet in the p. of Stoke-Lyne, Oxfordshire, 5 m. NNW of Bicester. Pop. 220.
FEWS, a parish of co. Waterford, 33 m. WNW of Kilmacthomas. Area 6,817 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,247; in 1841, 1,570.
FEWSTON, a parish and township in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. N of Otley, on the E bank of the Washburn. Area 1,660 acres. Pop. 2,118.
FEYE-OE, a small island on the W coast of Norway, in the diocese and 32 m. NW of Bergen, near the entrance of the Fens-Fiord, in N lat. 60° 46'.
FEYZIN, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Isere, cant. and com. of Saint-Simphorien-d'Ozon. Pop. 1,068.

FEZ, or Fas, a province of the empire of Marocco; bounded on the N by the prov. of Errif; on the E by Tedla; on the S by the mountains of Atlas; on the SW by Shawiya; and on the W by Gharb and Beni-hassan. Its dependencies are extensive, and include several mountainous tracts, well cultivated, and said to be well-peopled. Its principal mountains are Zaragh, one side of which is covered with vines, while the other is completely barren, and which lies between the river Sebu and the plain on which the cap. stands; and Zarkon or Zaraharum, which is shaded with olive-trees, and on the summit of which are the ruins of Titulies or Tuilit, once a considerable town, but demolished by a prince of the family of the Almoravides.—The Sebu, one of the largest rivers of W. Barbary, rises in the E part of the prov. near the foot of the Atlas mountains, and passes within 6 m. of the city of Fez. It is impassable, except in boats and rafts; and at Mamora, where it enters the ocean, is a deep and navigable river, capable of affording a cheap conveyance for corn to the city of F., which is at present supplied with that essential article by means of loaded camels, whose hire often exceeds the original cost of the grain. The river Bu-Regreb, also, which discharges itself into the ocean between the towns of Salli and Rabatt, rises on one of the mountains of Atlas, and flows through the woods and valleys of the territory of Fez.—The only other town in the province, of any note, besides the cap., is Mequinez. The whole of this prov. is a rich champaign country, remarkably productive in grain. The soil is a rich black or sometimes reddish mould, without stones or clay. No other manure is employed than the long stubble burned on the field; and no other culture is necessary than to throw the grain upon the ground, and cover it with the plough. The principal crops are wheat and barley; but on land adjacent to the rivers, beans, pease, caravancies, rice, and Indian corn, are occasionally cultivated. There are few trees, except the olive plantations and gardens around the cities of F. and Mequinez. Fruits of various kinds are abundant, particularly oranges, grapes, melons, and figs of different sorts. Cherries also are produced in this prov., though they are said not to ripen in any other part of the empire. In the country around the city of Mequinez, as well as in the prov. of Beni-hassan, is produced the tobacco called mequinasi, which is much esteemed for making snuff. A mineral salt of a red colour is dug from quarries in the vicinity of F.; and considerable quantities of saltpetre are also produced in the adjoining country. On the W side of the plain of taken by the Calvinists, and has since gradually de- | founder and first sovereign of F.; and this asylum, to which malefactors frequently betake themselves, is never violated by the emperor, or any other authority in his dominions. The country part of this prov. is inhabited altogether by Arabs, except a small tribe of Berbers.

FEZ, the first established Mahommedan sovereignty in W. Barbary, comprised, when a distinct kingdom, 7 provs.: viz., Fez, El-Gharb, El-rif, Beni-hassan, Temsena, Shawiya, and Tedla, now forming the N division of the empire of Marocco, and separated from the other parts of that empire by the river Morbeya on the S; on the E bounded by Algiers; and on the N and W by the sea. It is inhabited chiefly by Arabs, who dwell in tents, and are divided into various tribes; but the mountainous districts of Atlas are occupied by Berbers. The kingdom of F. owes its origin to Sidi-Idris, a descendant of Mahomet, who fled into Mauritania about the end of the 8th cent., to avoid the persecuting sword of the caliph Abd-Allah. He first settled at Tuilit, in the mountain of Zaaron or Zerone, between F. and Mequinez, where he acquired great influence among the natives. According to some authors, it was the son of this person, named also Idris, who founded the city and established the monarchy of F., which was for a long time called by Mahommedans the 'kingdom of the West.' The descendants of this prince continued to reign about 150 years; but during the 10th cent. divisions began to prevail, and various usurpers arose in the W of Barbary. The tribe of Zenetes, called Mequinesi, seized upon several provs., and founded the city of Mequinez, within 10 leagues of the cap.; and a marabout of that tribe having formed a considerable party in the prov. of Temsena, marched against Fez, and compelled the king to acknowledge the authority of the Zenetes at Mequinez. Another pretender, El- Mohadi, succeeded in expelling the princes of that family from their governments; and having declared himself caliph, marched towards Mount Atlas to extend his dominions. While thus engaged in the S, Al-Habid Almonsor, one of the generals of the king of Cordova, arrived with an army to aid the house of Idris; and having conquered a part of Fez, garrisoned the city of Arzilla, which remained for some time under the government of the Moors in Spain. El-Mohadi was unable to retain his usurped authority in Fez, and was obliged to pass into the E part of Africa. About the middle of the 11th cent., Abu Teshfin, chief of the Morabethuns or Morabites, a tribe which originated in the neighbourhood of Tunis, traversed Mount Atlas at the head of a numerous army, and took possession of the city of Agmet. Here he fixed his residence, and extending his conquests northwards, proclaimed himself Emir El-Mumenin, 'the chief of the faithful.' His arms were victorious; and he founded the dynasty of the Morabites, or, as they are termed by the Spanish writers, the Almoravides. He was succeeded in 1086 by his son Yussuf, who founded or rather finished the city of Maroksh or Marocco, which his father had begun, and there established the seat of his empire. Having entered the territories of the king of F. with a numerous army, and gained a decisive victory over his forces, he deprived him of his kingdom, and united it to the empire of Marocco, about the end of the 11th cent. -Modern Univ. Hist.-Chenier's Present State of Morocco, vol. ii. -Jackson's Account of Morocco. FEZ, EL-Fez, or Fas, a town of Marocco, the cap. of the above prov. and kingdom, finely situated on the Wad-el-Jubar, or 'Pearl river,' in N lat. 34° 6' 3", W long. 4° 58' 15", 197 m. NE of Marocco, and 122 m. E by S of Mehedia, at the mouth of the Wad-Sebu. It stands on the slope of several hills, which surround it on every side except the N and NE. It is surrounded by a high but old wall, about 4 m. in circuit; and has a fort at its E and another at its W extremity. Within the compass of the outer wall are some gardens and low houses forming suburbs. The river, which runs through the city, is divided into two streams; and is artificially conducted to almost every quarter of it. These canals supply the fountains, which are found in the courts of every principal house. The river itself falls into the Sebu, which passes to the N within 6 m. of the city. The city is divided into an old and a new town, the latter, or Fas-el-Bali, overlooking the former. The streets of Old F. are narrow and dark; so narrow that two men on horseback can hardly ride together; but those of the new city, or Fas-Jedide, are somewhat better laid out. The buildings, which have generally two floors, some of them three, are of brick or stone; often ornamented on the outside with mosaic work, and having small square grated windows toward the street. The flat roofs are ornamented with elevated towers. Their chief decorations are carving and gilding. Leo Africanus affirms, that the mosques Fez is a v. containing the sanctuary of Sidi Idris, the | and other religious edifices in this city were nearly 700 in number, of which 500 were magnificent buildings. At present they do not exceed 200, and the Caroubin or Carrauven, which is the most celebrated, appeared mean to Ali Bey after the Cathedral at Cordova. That traveller says nothing of the 900 lamps which were wont to burn every night in the temple, nor of the great lustre containing 150, made, it is said, from the bells which the kings of F. had conquered from Christian churches. He inquired for the complete manuscript of Livy, which has been said to exist in the library of this mosque; but his researches were vain, and he was afraid to discover much earnestness upon the subject lest he should render himself suspected. The state in which he found the books was such, that if any such manuscript existed, it has probably mouldered away, or been devoured by the rats. The Caroubin is remarkable for having a place where women may attend the public prayers, being the only place of worship in the Mahommedan world where a station is allowed them. The city contains 2 colleges, of which the decorations are expensive, but the resort of students is small. The system of education, however, is said to be preferable to that afforded by any other part of Barbary; to this place, therefore, the children of the rich are sent, to be instructed in whatever is reckoned agreeable or useful. "To form an idea of their manner of instruction," Ali Bey tells us, "we must imagine a man sitting cross-legged on the ground and singing in a lamentable tone, or uttering frightful cries, while fifteen or twenty youths sit in a circle round him, with their books or writing tables in their hands, and in complete discordance repeat his cries and songs. All their studies are confined to the Koran and its commentators, and so much grammar and logic as are necessary to understand what is intelligible in them." The city contains 100 public baths; some of them large and elegant. Several hospitals formerly existed here, but the emperor has seized the funds by which they were supported. People of different professions live in separate streets. The soil in the neighbourhood is good; but the oppressive government, by discouraging cultivation, has rendered the country a desert. The inhabitants, according to Jackson, amount to 360,000; Ali Bey estimated them at 100,000; the last plague being supposed to have carried off half the inhabitants. By some authorities the number is brought down as low as 30,000 souls; but Count Graberg, a recent and good authority, estimates them at 88,000, of whom 65,000 are Moors and Arabs; 10,000 Berbers and related tribes; 9,000 Jews; and 4,000 Negroes. The Mahommedans of Andalusia, Granada, and Cordova, who passed over, as above related, to F., introduced the Spanish method of dressing and dyeing sheep and goat skins red and yellow, then called Cordovan, now Morocco leather. At F., also, was first established the manufacture of milled woollen caps worn by the Moors, and brought to such perfection at Tunis. Besides gauzes, silks, and other stuffs fabricated at this city, it is celebrated for its manufacture of silk and gold sashes, of highly brilliant red, blue, lilac, and yellow colours. It has also some manufactories of pottery-ware and tiles; and of agricultural implements, such as ploughs and wooden spades; guns, daggers, spurs, saddlery, and gunpowder, are also made here. In addition to its own manufactures, F. is the common magazine of Barbary, to which are brought all kinds of commodities from the seaports of Marocco, from the E countries by the caravans of pilgrims, and from the centre of Africa by the caravans of merchants. Its chief exports are almonds, gums, raisins, dates, carraway and anise seeds, citrons, capers, olive-oil, honey, tallow, hides, * tanned leather, ostrich-feathers, lead ore, and elephants' teeth. To Timbuctu the merchants of F. send various articles of European, Indian, and Barbary produce, especially linens, muslins, fine cloths, raw silk, beads, brass nails, coffee, tea, and sugar, shawls, and sashes of silk and gold, haiks-long pieces of cloth used by the Africans as outer garments-of silk, cotton, and wool, turbans, spices, tobacco, and salt. In return they receive gums, gold rings, elephants' teeth, ambergris, ostrich-feathers, and slaves. The caravans which carry on this trade from F. to Timbuctu across the desert, generally travel 7 hours a-day at the rate of 31 m. an hour; and complete the journey between the two cities in 129 days, 54 only of which are employed in actual travelling. On account of the number of Mahommedan saints said to have been buried in F., it is considered by the Moors as a sacred asylum and an object of devotion. Hence all Jews and Christians are prohibited from entering its gates, and an order from the emperor is necessary before they can gain admission.
