This, however, seems to apply only to Old F., for the new town is principally occupied by Jews, who, notwithstanding the contempt with which they are treated, carry on a regular trade with the inhabitants of the city. In the various revolutions to which W. Barbary has been subject, the citizens of F. have always been ready to change their master, and generally yielded at the first approach of a victorious leader. The communication between F. and Mequinez is very easy and expeditious, by means of mules, which may be had ready saddled at all hours of the day, and which accomplish the journey at an easy pace in 6 hours, so as frequently to return the same day.—In 1799, a dreadful plague, which spread over all the empire of Marocco, originated in this city. It was ascribed by some to infected merchandise from the East, and by others to the pestilential smell of the dead locusts which had infested W. Barbary during the seven preceding years. In the cities of Old and New F. it carried off 1,200 or 1,500 persons daily, and during its continuance 65,000 of the inhabitants perished.
FEZA. See FATSA.
FEZAN-KHYLE, a village of Afghanistan, in the Largi valley, near the l. bank of the Kurum, 3 m. from the confluence of that river with the Indus, and 26 m. SW of Kala-Bagh.

FEZZAN, a tract of country in the interior of N. Africa, situated nearly midway between Tunis and Egypt, and tributary to Tripoli. It is considered as the country of the ancient Garamantes, and as corresponding with the Phazania of Pliny. As it is completely insulated by sandy or rocky tracts, and separated to a considerable distance from any other habitable district, it falls under the description of oases, or fertile spots in the middle of the Desert, and may be reckoned the largest oasis that is known. It is a circular, or rather oval territory, surrounded on all sides by an irregular ridge of mountains, except on the W border, where it communicates with the flat sandy desert or Sahara. The greatest length of the cultivated part of the kingdom is about 300 m. from N to S, and the greatest breadth 200 m. from E to W. But the mountainous region of Harutch, on the E frontier, and other desert districts of considerable extent towards the S and W, are within its territory. On the N it is bordered by territories held by Arab tribes, nominally dependent on Tripoli; on the E by the hilly deserts already mentioned; on the S and SE by the country of the Tibbus; on the SW by that of the nomadic Tuariks; and on the W by Arab territory. Almost the only historical notices of this country by the ancients are to be found in Pliny, who mentions it as one of the most important conquests of the Roman general Balbus .- The climate | is at no season temperate or agreeable. The heat is intense during summer; and when the wind blows from the S, is scarcely supportable even by the natives. A bleak N wind prevails during winter, which produces a severe and chilling degree of cold. Rain very seldom falls, and when it does come, is little in quantity; but water, notwithstanding, is found everywhere at 10 or 15 ft. depth,—a circumstance supposed to be owing to the high lands by which the country is surrounded. Thunder and lightning are rare; but storms of wind, whirling up sand and dust, are very frequent. The Sudah or Black mountains appear to be composed of basalt, nearly black, the surface of which has acquired a glassy or polished aspect. They rise to an elevation of about 1,500 ft., are situated S from Sockna, and extend 100 m. in breadth from N to S. They are perfectly barren, of irregular form, occasionally broken into detached masses, and sometimes rising in cones. The latitude of this chain, as ascertained by Captain Lyon, is from 28° 40' to 27° 30'. There is no river or stream of any note in the whole country, so far as was observed by Horneman; but Edrisi mentions a river of some size, which takes its course by Zuela, and which is lost in the sand before it reaches the sea. The soil is a deep sand, covering calcareous rock or earth, and sometimes a stratum of argillaceous substance; but as the springs are so abundant, few regions in the N of Africa exhibit a richer vegetation. Salt and nitre effloresce on the surface in many quarters. Both the soil and climate are well-adapted for the growth of wheat, maize, and barley; but from the indolence of the people, their ignorance of tillage, and the oppression of their government, a sufficiency of corn for their subsistence is seldom raised in the country, and they depend upon importations from the Arab countries to the N. Pot-herb and garden-vegetables in general are plentiful, and some senna is raised in the W districts; but the natural and staple produce of F. is dates; figs, pomegranates, and lemons, are abundant.—There are few horses in the country, and camels are kept only by the wealthy inhabitants; asses are generally used for all the purposes of burden, draught, or carriage. A few horned cattle are found in the fertile districts; they are employed in drawing water from the wells, and are never slaughtered for food unless in cases of extreme necessity. The ordinary domestic animal is the goat; a few hairy broad-tailed sheep are reared in the S parts of the kingdom. The antelope, hyena, gazelle, jackall, tiger-cat, panther, and ostrich, are the principal wild animals; snakes, adders, and scorpions, are numerous. Commerce.] F. is the most advantageously situated of all the inland countries in Africa for the purposes of commerce, as it lies in the shortest and most convenient line of communication between the Mediterranean and the centre of Africa, as well as between W. Africa, Egypt, and Arabia. Its trade is in fact considerable, and its inhabitants are the most enterprising merchants in that quarter of the globe; but their traffic consists chiefly in foreign merchandise, and they are enriched by the carrying trade across the deserts. The capital, Murzuk, is the great resort of numerous caravans from Cairo, Bengasi, Gadames, Troat, and Sudan, and the rendezvous of Africa on their way to Mecca. The caravans from the S and W bring, as articles of commerce, slaves of both sexes, ostrich-feathers, tiger-skins, zibette, and gold, partly in dust, and partly in native grains, to be manufactured into ornaments for the inhabitants of the interior. From Bornu copper is imported in great quantities; from Cairo, silks, calicoes, cloths, and E. India goods; tobacco, snuff, and Turkey-ware, from Bengasi; paper, fire-arms, sabres, red worsted caps, and woollen cloths, from Tripoli and Gadames; and butter, oil, corn, senna, and camels, from the Tuariks and southern Arabs. See article MURZUK. Payments are generally made in gold dust, of which the value is always expressed by weight. The only current coin is Spanish silver piastres. There are no articles of manufacture produced in the country, and the natives discover little ingenuity as artificers. The only tradesmen are shoemakers and smiths; the latter work every metal without distinction, so that the same person who forges shoes for the sultan's horse forms rings for the princesses. The women, indeed, make a coarse woollen cloth called abbe; but, so imperfect is their manufacturing skill, that the whole work is performed solely by the hand, and the woof is inserted into the warp thread by thread. Government.] F. is governed by a sultan, who reigns with unlimited authority over his subjects, but holds his dominions as tributary to the pasha of Tripoli, to whom he pays an annual tribute of 15,000 dollars. The crown is hereditary; but does not always descend from father to son. The law of succession requires, that, when a vacancy occurs, the oldest prince of the blood royal shall ascend the throne,—a regulation which frequently occasions an appeal to the sword. The sultan was, until 1811, of the family of the Sherifs of Waden or Zuila. The palace is situated within the fortress of the cap. The sultan never appears without the castle walls, except on Fridays, when he goes to the great mosque, or on other public festivals, when he is attended by his whole court. On these days of solemnity he rides on horseback, in a plain on the outside of the town, where his courtiers exhibit their skill in equestrian exercises. The state apparel consists of a large white stuff frock, made in the Sudan fashion, and brocaded with gold and silver. Under this covering he wears the ordinary dress of the Tripolitans, without any thing remarkable in his appearance, except his turban, which extends a yard from the front to the hinder part, and is two-thirds of a yard in breadth. Justice is administered by an officer named the caid, who is at the same time the chief of the clergy, and possesses great influence with the people. His decisions are directed by the Mahommedan law and acknowledged customs. In criminal cases the judgment is arbitrary, or is referred to the sultan. The dignity of chief justice is hereditary in a certain family; but the sultan selects the individual of that race who is most qualified by his learning to fill the office, or, in other words, who can best read or write. The princes of the sultan's family also claim a right of jurisdiction, and of imposing corporal punishments. Next to the cadi, as head of the clergy, is the great iman.—The armed force amounts to about 5,000 men.—The public revenues arise from territorial domains of the crown, from the royal gardens and forests, from salt pools and natron lakes, from duties on foreign trade, embracing a tax of 2 dollars on every slave entering his dominions, from assessments on all gardens and cultivated lands, and from arbitrary fines or requisitions. The princes of the royal family are supported by proportions of corn delivered weekly from the sultan's stores, by the proceeds of territory appropriated to that purpose, and by occasional exactions from the people, levied by their personal authority, and by their own slaves. The cadi, the great officers of the government, and the clergy, are maintained by the produce of date-tree woods and gardens, granted as temporary possessions to those who hold the respective offices. Divisions and Towns.] The kingdom of F. contains 101 towns and villages, among which there are few places of any note, and still fewer whose positions are ascertained. The districts are Murzuk containing the cap.; on the E side, Hofrah, Shargiah, and Foghah; on the N side, Sibha-Bananes, Jofra, and Shatti; on the W side, Wadi-Shargi, Wadi-Gurbi, and Wadi-Atbah; on the S side, Ghatrun. These names are likewise the names of the 12 principal towns, in each of which there is a resident caid. Sochna is not usually considered a part of F. Jerma, or Yerma, unquestionably the Garama of the Romans, and the cap. of the country at the time of its being subdued by their arms, is situated as far to the W as Zuila is to the E of Murzuk, and is full of majestic ruins and ancient inscriptions. Temissa, about 120 m. E of the cap., is rather a garrison than a town. Katron, or Gatrun, about 60 m. S of Murzuk, is remarkable only for the multitude of domestic fowls reared by its inhabitants, and the abundant crops of Indian corn in its neighbourhood. Mendra, about 60 m. SE of the cap., is an inconsiderable place. Teghery, about 70 m. SW of the cap., is a small town, nearest to the W frontier. To the N are Sibha, Hun, and Waden. Population.] The pop. of F. is calculated at 70,000 or 75,000 souls by some, but is raised to double that number by others. It is mainly composed of Tuariks and Tibbus. Some are as black as Negroes, others as white as the Moors on the coast; others olive, yellow, or brown. They all profess the Mahommedan religion. The genuine natives are described as a people of ordinary stature, deep brown complexion, short black hair, regular features, and feeble limbs. Their whole appearance and gesture is said to denote want of energy either in mind or body, which may be ascribed partly to the oppressive nature of their government, and partly to the extreme poverty of their diet, which consists chiefly of dates, and a kind of farinaceous pap, with sometimes, though rarely, a little rancid oil or fat. But though remarkably abstemious in diet, they are greatly addicted to drunkenness. Their beverage is the juice of the date-tree, called lugibi, which, when fresh, is sweet and pleasant, or a liquor prepared from dates called bussa, which is extremely intoxicating. The manners of the females, in general, are licentious, and they are vehemently fond of amusement, especially of dancing in the open places of the towns and villages. Their habitations are as wretched as their subsistence. They are extremely low, with no other aperture for light than the door, and are built of palm branches, or with stones or bricks of a calcareous earth mixed with clay, and dried in the sun. The walls are covered over with white mortar; and the whole operations of building and plastering are performed without tools, entirely by the hands of the labourer.—The dress of the F. consists of a shirt or frock generally blue, made of a coarse linen or cotton cloth, brought from Cairo, and the abbe of their own manufacture. The richer class wear the Tripolitan habit, with a Sudan shirt over it of variegated pattern and colours. The ornaments of the women consist chiefly in necklaces of glass beads, or pieces of agate with a round silver plate in the front, trinkets made of silver bells, coral and amber suspended from the tresses of hair on the head, and rings of glass, horn, brass, or silver, to the number sometimes of 9 or 10 on each arm and leg.—The principal diseases of the natives, are a fever and ague which is particularly dangerous to foreigners; hæmorrhoids or piles, which are supposed to be aggravated by the immoderate use of red pepper; the small-pox; and the venereal disorder. Their surgical art extends to the setting of a simple fracture and occasional blood-letting, which is always done by cupping, and never by venesection; and their practice of medicine is confined almost entirely to amulets, consisting of sentences from the Koran, written on a slip of paper, which the patient wears about his neck, and is sometimes compelled to swallow.
FIA, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Tarn, cant. of Saint-Paul-Cap-de-Joux. Pop. 1,530.
FIADANE, a town of Madagascar, in the district of Mahavelona, 100 m. W of Tamatave, on a mountain. It is surrounded by a ditch 60 m. in breadth.
FIAMIGNANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Ultra, district and 15 m. SE of Citta- Ducale.
FIANO, a town of the Pontifical states, comarca and 22 m. NNE of Rome, near the r. bank of the Tiber.—Also a mandamento and village of Piedmont, in the prov. and 15 m. NW of Turin. Pop. 885. FID
FIANONA, or FLANONA, a small maritime town of Illyria, in the gov. of Trieste, circle and 19 m. SW of Fiûme, on the gulf of Quarnero. Pop. 810. Chestnuts form one of its chief articles of export.
FIATTONE, a village of the duchy of Lucca, in the district and 11 m. NNW of Borgo-a-Mozzano.
FICARRA, a town of Sicily, in the district and 9 m. WSW of Patti, and 40 m. WSW of Messina.
FICARIZZA, a maritime village of Sicily, in the prov. and 15 m. ESE of Palermo. Pop. 1,820.
